behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser/row-index handoff -> schema validator -> DLQ writer. A unit test of only validator logic can miss row-number propagation bugs across modules. No real OS/network/subprocess is required; in-process wiring with fake S3 input and test DLQ sink is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run pipeline components together on a small CSV fixture with known missing required fields at specific line numbers; assert each invalid row lands in DLQ with the exact original row number and valid rows continue through normal path.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is pure transformation logic for date parsing/normalization. Real failure is inside one function/object boundary (format detection + canonicalization), with no required module handoff, infra, or external dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Table-driven tests over representative ISO 8601, US, and European date strings (including ambiguous and invalid samples), asserting exact canonical output or explicit rejection.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: This is a real runtime/resource behavior: timeout and memory-exhaustion failures emerge from actual process memory, GC behavior, and end-to-end I/O throughput under load. In-process mocks can hide OOM and timing pathologies.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the pipeline as a real process against a generated 500k-row CSV and real persistence target used in test env; enforce configured timeout and capture peak memory usage, asserting completion before timeout and no OOM/kill.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is retry-policy + ingestion client + batch orchestrator interaction. A deterministic stub S3 client returning 503 then success can expose whether retries/backoff are applied and batch continues. Real network is not required for this behavior itself.
behaviors.test_description: Wire ingestion service with a controllable S3 client double that returns 503 for first N attempts then success; assert retry count, backoff schedule progression, and that batch eventually completes instead of hard-failing.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text is explicitly about retry/backoff control flow on 503, which is reproducible with deterministic client responses; no real network condition is required to validate this contract.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: Core failure is state/checkpoint/idempotency handoff across pipeline modules plus persistent DB state. This requires real module wiring with a real local PostgreSQL instance, but can be tested without OS-level subprocess orchestration by re-instantiating pipeline state in test.
behaviors.test_description: Process part of a batch, persist checkpoint, simulate restart by creating a new pipeline instance, resume same input against test PostgreSQL, then assert no duplicate rows (by keys/count) and exactly-once semantics for already committed records.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior does not require validating OS signal handling or true process manager semantics; re-instantiation against persistent DB is sufficient to catch duplication faults.
